trade.summary: Bilateral trade is limited given the structural similarity of both economies (hydrocarbon exporters). Russia exports wheat, military equipment, and industrial goods to Saudi Arabia. Saudi Arabia exports petrochemicals and some consumer goods to Russia. Both countries compete in global oil markets but coordinate through OPEC+. Russia-Saudi trade has grown since Western sanctions reduced Russia's trade with Europe.
disputes: Periodic tensions over OPEC+ production quotas
Price war episode in 2020 before OPEC+ deal

diplomatic.status: pragmatic partnership — energy cooperation with underlying strategic competition
diplomatic.summary: Russia-Saudi Arabia relations are characterized by pragmatic energy diplomacy through the OPEC+ framework, coexisting with fundamental strategic differences (Iran, Syria, Yemen, broader Middle East influence). Saudi Arabia has not joined Western sanctions against Russia. Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman has maintained communication with Putin. Saudi Arabia has positioned itself as a diplomatic bridge, including hosting Ukraine-related peace discussions in 2023. Both are authoritarian states with transactional foreign policies.
recent_incidents: Saudi Arabia hosted Ukraine-Russia peace talks in Jeddah (2023)
OPEC+ production cut coordination maintained throughout Ukraine conflict
Saudi Arabia declined to join Western sanctions against Russia

military.summary: No formal military alliance. Both have opposing positions in key conflicts — Russia supports Assad in Syria while Saudi Arabia backed Syrian opposition; Russia is Iran's partner while Saudi Arabia views Iran as its primary regional threat. Russia has sold some military equipment to Saudi neighbors. Saudi Arabia's primary security partner remains the United States. Strategic competition in Middle East outweighs military cooperation.
military.alliance_status: none — strategic competition in Middle East with selective coordination
incidents: Competing proxy positions in Syria, Yemen, Lebanon

financial.summary: Saudi Arabia has avoided sanctioning Russia, enabling some financial flows that Western countries have cut. Saudi investment funds have not divested from some Russia-adjacent assets. The petrodollar debate (Saudi Arabia potentially pricing oil in non-dollar currencies) has geopolitical implications. Trade finance has adapted to bypass Western banking restrictions.

energy.summary: Energy is the defining dimension of the relationship. Russia and Saudi Arabia are the two largest oil producers in OPEC+. Their coordination (or lack thereof) moves global oil markets. The OPEC+ alliance, forged in 2016, represents the most consequential bilateral energy cooperation in the world. Both countries use production coordination as a geopolitical tool. Despite being competitors in selling oil to the same customers, the coordination has been largely sustained.

scientific.summary: Limited scientific collaboration. Some exchanges in energy technology, particularly in oil production techniques. Russian nuclear energy company Rosatom has expressed interest in the Saudi nuclear program, though Saudi Arabia has also engaged the US and South Korea. Academic exchanges are modest.
